summary: Deprecated audit trail for DisableLowQosTimerResolution. The current app writes HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Kernel\DisableLowQosTimerResolution = 1, and the mirror plus decompiled kernel path now show the explicit low-QoS timer-resolution gate even though this research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft source for the exact registry key and value semantics.

setting.name: Disable Low QoS Timer Resolution
setting.casual_explanation: This appears to be a low-level kernel switch related to how Windows handles low-QoS timer resolution behavior. It is not a normal user setting.
setting.why_people_change_it: People would only touch it when reproducing timer-resolution experiments or undoing manual registry changes from advanced tweak guides.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Timer-resolution behavior affects power usage, responsiveness, and how often the system wakes to service timers. Microsoft documents the general timer-resolution tradeoff, but this research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft source for the exact registry key the app writes.
setting.professional_notes: The repo research notes list DisableLowQosTimerResolution = 1 and associate it with KeDisableLowQosTimerResolution. Microsoft documents that increasing timer resolution can reduce overall system performance and power efficiency, and it publishes adjacent timer-resolution controls such as timeBeginPeriod and QosTimerResolution. The mirrored decompiled routine PspSetProcessTimerResolutionPolicy.c shows the kernel gate directly, but this research pass still did not capture a primary Microsoft page that documents the kernel registry value DisableLowQosTimerResolution itself. The record therefore treats the app write as observed repo provenance plus kernel pseudocode corroboration.

allowed_values.label: Primary Microsoft mapping not captured
allowed_values.meaning: This research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft source for the exact DisableLowQosTimerResolution registry semantics.

allowed_values.label: Observed app profile
allowed_values.meaning: The current app treats 1 as the default low-QoS timer-resolution behavior, based on repo research rather than a captured primary Microsoft source.

targets.notes: Tracked as an observed kernel registry mapping only.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes DisableLowQosTimerResolution = 1, but this research pass has not yet captured a primary Microsoft source for the value itself.

windows_defaults.label: Conservative baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Systems where this dataset has not yet validated DisableLowQosTimerResolution from a primary Microsoft source

states.rationale: Do not publish a validated timer-behavior baseline until the exact registry mapping is backed by a primary Microsoft source.

windows_defaults.notes: This keeps the low-level timer-behavior setting out of published presets until it is properly sourced.

recommended_profiles.label: Hold for research

states.rationale: Do not publish or apply the value until a primary Microsoft source is captured.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to document the timer-behavior setting without overstating confidence.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a primary-source Microsoft-backed kernel mapping for low-QoS timer resolution behavior.
tradeoffs: The timer-resolution feature area is documented honestly.
The kernel registry value stays blocked from validated publication.

The exact registry semantics remain based on repo provenance rather than a captured primary source.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: timeBeginPeriod function
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents the general timer-resolution tradeoff, noting that higher resolution can reduce overall system performance and power efficiency. The page confirms the feature area but does not publish the DisableLowQosTimerResolution registry key.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Timer Resolution
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents timer resolution as a system behavior that affects timing precision and system cost, but the page does not publish the DisableLowQosTimerResolution registry key.

evidence.title: nohuto mirror: low-QoS timer resolution gate
evidence.summary: The decompiled kernel routine checks KeDisableLowQosTimerResolution before toggling the process policy bit and calling ExpUpdateTimerResolution, and the mirror docs record the same DisableLowQosTimerResolution = 1 gate.

evidence.title: Repo system research notes for kernel registry values
evidence.summary: The repo documents DisableLowQosTimerResolution = 1 as personal research and ties it to KeDisableLowQosTimerResolution.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes DisableLowQosTimerResolution = 1 for the current tweak.

decision.why: Official Microsoft docs confirm the timer-resolution feature area and its tradeoffs, but this research pass did not capture a primary Microsoft source for the DisableLowQosTimerResolution kernel registry key.
blocking_issues: No primary Microsoft source for DisableLowQosTimerResolution was captured in this research pass.
The current app mapping relies on repo provenance instead of a directly captured Microsoft source.

open_questions: Can a primary Microsoft source for DisableLowQosTimerResolution be captured?
Should the app surface this only as an experimental kernel timer setting?

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: if ( KeDisableLowQosTimerResolution ) { ... if ( a2 ) { ... } else { ... } ... ExpUpdateTimerResolution(0, 0, 0LL); }
validation_proof.notes: Timer-resolution audit trail. The mirror docs record DisableLowQosTimerResolution = 1, and the decompiled kernel routine shows the gate controlling the policy bit and timer-resolution refresh path.
